claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

- Is your child **under 16 years old**?
- Is your child **16 to 19 years old**?
- Is your child **20 years old or over**?

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to check, please let me know their ages as well.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I'd like to check for both of my children. I have two kids - Alex is 5 years old and Blake is 10 years old. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thank you for providing those details! Since both Alex (5 years old) and Blake (10 years old) are under 16, they both automatically qualify in terms of age. Let me continue the assessment for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about work status:**

Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

Given their ages (5 and 10), I'd assume they're not in paid employment, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not working at all - they're just kids! Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10, so neither of them has a job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - they're both young children. So the answer is "yes" - they are both working less than 24 hours per week (in fact, not working at all). Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about benefits:**

Are Alex and Blake **not** receiving certain benefits in their own right? 

Specifically, this means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

Given their ages (5 and 10), it would be very unusual for them to be receiving these benefits in their own name. Can you confirm that neither child is receiving these benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - neither Alex nor Blake receives any benefits in their own name. They're just regular kids, no benefits or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about apprenticeships:**

Are Alex and Blake **not** in any apprenticeship programmes?

Given their ages (5 and 10), I expect they wouldn't be in any apprenticeships, but I just need to confirm that neither child is currently enrolled in an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're way too young for apprenticeships! Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10, so they're both just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes complete sense! They're both in primary school, so they're not in any apprenticeships. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check your residency status.**

Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements? You only need to meet **one** of these:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You usually reside in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You live abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - we live in Manchester. So option 1 applies to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Since you normally live in the UK (in Manchester), you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.**

Are you responsible for both children? You meet this requirement if **any** of the following apply:

1. **Living with the child** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live with me at home. So option 1 applies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since both Alex and Blake live with you, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement for both children. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about other claimants:**

Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

This could be:
- Another parent
- A grandparent
- Another family member or guardian

Please let me know if:
1. **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for either child
2. **Someone else was claiming**, but you've agreed that you should be the one to claim instead
3. **There's a dispute** about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to check if any special circumstances apply to where Alex and Blake are living.**

Are either of the children currently:

1. **In hospital** - Staying in hospital for treatment
2. **In care** - In local authority care
3. **Fostered** - Being fostered by someone else
4. **Living normally at home** - Just living at home with you in a normal arrangement

From what you've told me, it sounds like both children are living normally at home with you in Manchester. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - both Alex and Blake just live at home with me. Nothing special, just a normal family situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, thank you for confirming! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, are you fully supporting both children without the local council contributing to their housing or living costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the local council isn't paying anything towards them. I'm supporting both Alex and Blake myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's the final question. Let me now provide you with the outcome of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Based on the information you've provided, I can now give you the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News - You Are ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit!

Based on the assessment, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary for Each Child:

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| **Alex** | 5 | ✅ Yes |
| **Blake** | 10 | ✅ Yes |

### Why You Qualify:
Both children meet all the eligibility criteria:
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not in paid work
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- ✅ You normally live in the UK
- ✅ Both children live with you
- ✅ No one else is claiming for them
- ✅ Local council is not paying towards their care

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

### Important Information:

- **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
- ⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through your tax return

### Useful Link:
🔗 [How to Claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, (2) They are not working, (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) They are not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK, (6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living at home in a normal arrangement, and (9) The local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 10) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, (2) They are not working, (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) They are not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK, (6) You are responsible for Blake as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living at home in a normal arrangement, and (9) The local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They normally reside in the UK (Manchester), both children are under 16 and live with them in a normal family arrangement, neither child is working or receiving benefits, no one else is claiming for either child, and the local council is not contributing to their care. All eligibility criteria are satisfied for both Alex and Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, (2) They are not working, (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) They are not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK, (6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living at home in a normal arrangement, and (9) The local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 10) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, (2) They are not working, (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) They are not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK, (6) You are responsible for Blake as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living at home in a normal arrangement, and (9) The local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They normally reside in the UK (Manchester), both children are under 16 and live with them in a normal family arrangement, neither child is working or receiving benefits, no one else is claiming for either child, and the local council is not contributing to their care. All eligibility criteria are satisfied for both Alex and Blake.
